context: 'They only consult to cast him down from his excellency. 'They delight in lies. 'They bless with their mouth, 'but they curse inwardly, Selah.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 'My soul, wait thou only upon God, 'for my expectation is from him.'
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 'He only is my rock and my salvation. 'He is my defense. 'I shall not be moved.
point_summary: Verse 6 nearly mirrors verse 2, a deliberate repetition the sermon highlights to underscore that God alone as rock, salvation, and defense is the psalm's immovable anchor.

context: 'In God is my salvation and my glory. 'The rock of my strength and my refuge is in God.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 'Trust in him at all times, ye people. 'Pour out your heart before him. 'God is a refuge for us, Selah.
point_summary: Psalm 62:8's command to "pour out your heart before him" is drawn from the middle of the verse as the sermon's governing theme, anchoring the call to bring one's deepest burdens directly to God.

context: 'Surely men of low degree are vanity, 'and men of high degree are a lie. 'To be laid in the balance, 'they are altogether lighter than vanity.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 'Trust not in oppression, 'and become not vain in robbery. 'If riches increase, set not your heart upon them.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 'God hath spoken once, twice have I heard this, 'that power belongeth unto God.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 'Also unto thee, O Lord, belongeth mercy, 'for thou renderest to every man according to his work.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: We find that the psalmist back in Psalm 62, he's dealing with some trials.
point_summary: Psalm 62 is read as a model of how a believer responds to trials, with the psalmist's testimony of waiting on God emerging as the fruit of his suffering.

context: The Bible says in verse number one, truly my soul waiteth upon God.
point_summary: Amid the trials of life, the psalmist's first testimony is a resolved, deliberate waiting upon God alone—a discipline the sermon presents as difficult but necessary in the Christian life.

context: In verse number five he repeats, my soul wait thou only upon God.
point_summary: Repeating the resolve of verse 1, verse 5 shows the psalmist's testimony rising from his trial: a deliberate, renewed command to his own soul to wait on God alone.

context: Be still, the psalmist said, be still and know that I am God.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: truly my soul waiteth upon God
point_summary: Waiting on God is not passive resignation but a resolved trust, grounded in the conviction that salvation comes *from Him* alone—not from hurrying the process.

context: how long will you imagine mischief against a man?
point_summary: Even amid ongoing trial, the psalmist's question "how long?" in verse 3 frames the surrounding context of waiting, showing that the resolve to wait on God is forged in the middle of real and prolonged suffering.

context: my soul wait thou only upon God
point_summary: Psalm 62:5's shift from indicative to imperative ("my soul wait thou only upon God") reflects the psalmist's deliberate self-talk, resolving to wait on God even in the midst of trial.

reference_text: from him, from God, cometh my salvation
context: He's saying, I understand that from him, from God, cometh my salvation.
point_summary: Even amid trial, David's declaration that salvation comes *from him*—from God—reflects a hard-won resolve to wait on God rather than take matters into his own hands.

context: 'For my expectation is from him.'
point_summary: Genuine expectation directed solely toward God—not self-effort or manipulation—is the posture David models, and the sermon presses listeners to identify what they are trusting God alone to accomplish.

context: in the Lord put I my trust
point_summary: Psalm 11:1's declaration of trust in the Lord is cited as a parallel witness to Psalm 62, reinforcing that wholehearted, unconditional trust in God is grounded in knowing his trustworthy character.

context: and they that know thy name will put their trust in thee
point_summary: Knowing God's name — knowing who He is and that He is trustworthy — is the ground given in Psalm 9:10 for why believers can trust Him at all times.

context: trust him at all times, but he says, pour out your heart before him.
point_summary: Pouring out one's heart before God is grounded in the reality that Christ, as a sympathetic high priest who suffered and was tempted, is both able and willing to help those who come to him transparently.

context: Psalm 55, cast thy burden upon the Lord. He will sustain thee.
point_summary: Casting burdens on the Lord, as Psalm 55:22 instructs, is presented as the scriptural basis for the call to pour out one's heart before God, who sustains and cares for his people.

context: Trust not in oppression and become not vain in robbery: if riches increase, set not your heart upon them.
point_summary: Verse 10 stands in deliberate contrast to verse 8's call to trust God at all times, warning against placing confidence in oppression, robbery, or increasing riches, which cannot ultimately fix human problems.

context: God hath spoken once, twice have I heard this, that power belongeth unto God.
point_summary: God's omnipotence is underscored by the psalmist's formula—spoken once, heard twice—confirming that all power belongs to God alone, a truth Jesus himself claimed in the New Testament.

context: I wonder tonight, being to look at Psalm 62, will we take the instruction of the psalmist, will we just be resolved that we're going to wait on God and trust Him at all times?
point_summary: Psalm 62 is presented as a call to resolved trust—waiting on God, pouring out the heart before Him, and acknowledging total dependence on the One who alone holds all power and mercy.

context: as we look at Psalm 119, I like to think and act biblically.
point_summary: Psalm 119 is invoked as the standard for thinking and acting biblically, illustrating the preacher's aspiration to be so immersed in Scripture that his mind instinctively interprets life through God's Word.

context: the heavens declare the glory of God and the firmament showeth forth his handiwork
point_summary: While creation declares God's glory (Ps. 19:1), the sermon draws a contrast, arguing that the full revelation of God comes through Scripture, not nature alone.

context: David says, I have been young and now I'm old, yet have I not seen the righteous forsaken nor his seed begging bread.
point_summary: David's testimony that he never saw the righteous forsaken nor begging bread is cited as assurance that God faithfully provided for his people even through Egypt's severe famine.

context: The heavens declare the glory of God. The firmament showeth forth his handiwork.
point_summary: General revelation through the heavens declares God's existence but cannot communicate the gospel, which God has entrusted specifically to redeemed people to proclaim.

context: The psalmist says in Psalm 119, it was good for me to be afflicted.
point_summary: Psalm 119:71's confession that affliction was good because it produced growth in God's statutes illustrates that God's will for saved people includes using hardship as a means of spiritual maturation.

context: great is the Lord and greatly to be praised, as we sang from Psalm 48, verses one and two
point_summary: God's greatness, sung from Psalm 48:1 throughout 2023, frames the sermon's year-long theme of discovering what makes God great, here focused specifically on the greatness of his mercy.

context: great is the Lord and greatly to be praised, as we sang from Psalm 48, verses one and two
point_summary: Psalm 48:1–2 supplied the congregational song sung throughout 2023 to anchor the year-long theme that the Lord is great and greatly to be praised.

context: the psalmist reveals that he's trying out to the Lord
point_summary: In a season of trouble, the psalmist models honest, transparent crying out to God, setting the stage for the recollection of divine mercy as the ground of hope.

context: He's in a day of trouble
point_summary: In a day of trouble, the psalmist's honest cry to God illustrates the kind of transparent, struggling prayer that leads ultimately to recalling God's mercy as the ground of hope.

context: I call to remembrance my song in the night. I commune with mine own heart, my spirit made diligent search.
point_summary: In seasons of trouble, honest recollection of God's past mercies—voiced transparently before Him—becomes the turning point from despair toward renewed hope.

context: will the Lord cast off forever? And will He be favorable no more?
point_summary: Psalm 77:7's question — "Is his mercy clean gone forever?" — is cited as an example of the psalmist's raw, transparent wrestling with God in seasons of trouble, contrasting with believers who suppress such honest struggle.

context: Is His mercy clean gone forever? Doth His promise fail forevermore?
point_summary: In seasons of deep trouble, the believer may honestly question whether God's mercy has utterly ceased — and Psalm 77:8 is cited as a model of that raw, transparent wrestling before God.

context: Hath God forgotten to be gracious? Hath He in anger shut up His tender mercies?
point_summary: In seasons of deep trouble, the psalmist's raw, transparent questions about whether God's mercy is "clean gone forever" model an honest wrestling with God that believers often suppress rather than voice.

context: and I said, this is my infirmity, but I will remember the years of the right hand of the Most High. I will remember the works of the Lord. Surely, I will remember thy wonders of old.
point_summary: When doubt and difficulty tempt the believer to question God's mercy, the psalmist models a deliberate turning—naming his infirmity but choosing to remember the past works and wonders of the Most High rather than continuing down that path.

context: I will remember the works of the Lord. Surely, I will remember thy wonders of old.
point_summary: When doubts assail and it feels as though God's mercies have dried up, the psalmist's deliberate choice to remember God's past works becomes the turning point that breaks the downward spiral of despair.

context: I will meditate also of all thy work and talk of all thy doings.
point_summary: Deliberate remembrance of God's past works and wonders is the psalmist's chosen remedy when circumstances tempt him to conclude that God's mercy has ceased.

context: Thy way, O God, is in the sanctuary. Who is so great a God as our God?
point_summary: God's way being "in the sanctuary" (Ps. 77:13) anchors the psalmist's turn from despairing questions about God's mercy to deliberate remembrance of His past works and wonders.

context: Thou art the God that doest wonders. Thou hast declared thy strength among the people.
point_summary: Psalm 77:14's declaration that God "doest wonders" anchors the psalmist's recovery from despair, modeling how deliberate remembrance of God's past works counters the lie that His mercies have dried up.

context: I'm thankful that our God knows our frame. I'm thankful that he knows that we're but dust.
point_summary: God's gracious accommodation of Gideon's wavering faith illustrates that He knows our frame and that we are but dust, meeting human weakness with repeated encouragement rather than condemnation.

context: Remember Psalm 46, be still and know that I'm God.
point_summary: Gideon's act of worship before battle illustrates the call of Psalm 46:10 to "be still" amid busyness and recognize God's sovereign work before pressing forward.

context: The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want.
point_summary: David's declaration that "the Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want" frames the entire psalm as the foundation for the sermon's central claim that the believer's cup is not half full or half empty but overflowing.

context: He maketh me to lie down in green pastures, he leadeth me beside the still waters.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: He restoreth my soul, he leadeth me in the paths of righteousness for his name's sake.
point_summary: God's restorative work and righteous leading of His people are presented as foundational blessings of the Shepherd's care, read here as part of the psalm's cumulative portrait of overflowing divine provision.

context: Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, for thou art with me. Thy rod, thy staff, they comfort me.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Thou preparest a table before me in the presence of mine enemies. Thou anointest my head with oil, my cup runneth over.
point_summary: God's provision for His people is not partial but overflowing — the cup of the believer is neither half full nor half empty, but running over entirely.

context: Surely goodness and mercy shall follow me all the days of my life, and I will dwell in the house of the Lord forever.
point_summary: Psalm 23:6's closing promise of dwelling in the Lord's house forever anchors the entire psalm's portrait of overflowing divine provision, introduced here as the sermon's culminating focus.

context: my cup runneth over
point_summary: God's overflowing goodness to His people—not merely a full cup but a running-over one—should shape the believer's daily perspective and answer to life's ordinary questions.

context: thou preparest a table before me, what? In the presence of mine enemies. In the presence of mine enemies.
point_summary: Even amid enemies and life's unchanging trials, the overflowing cup of Psalm 23:5 reflects biblical truths that circumstances and feelings cannot alter, grounding the believer's perspective in what God has secured.

context: But yet David was able to say, my cup runneth over. My cup runneth over.
point_summary: Even amid enemies and life's unchanging trials, the overflowing cup of Psalm 23:5 reflects biblical realities—Christ's love, heaven's certainty, the Shepherd's leadership—that circumstances and feelings cannot alter.

context: my shepherd leads me. My shepherd leads me.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Here in Psalm 23, in verse number three, the Bible says, he restoreth my soul.
point_summary: God's leadership through the Shepherd — restoring the soul and guiding in paths of righteousness — is the believer's anchor when personal direction and understanding fail.

context: We'll look at verse number two. He maketh me to lie down in green pastures. Notice he leadeth me beside the still waters.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: He restoreth my soul. He leadeth me in the
point_summary: God's leadership through the Shepherd is the believer's anchor when direction is lacking, as Psalm 23:3's "he leadeth me" assures that the sheep need not rely on their own understanding.

reference_text: thy word is a lamp unto my feet and it's a light unto my path
context: And he says, thy word is a lamp unto my feet and it's a light unto my path.
point_summary: God's Word functions as directional light for each immediate step of the journey, not illuminating the entire path at once, just as headlights reveal only enough road ahead to reach the destination.

context: Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil: for thou art with me; thy rod and thy staff they comfort me.
point_summary: Even in a sin-cursed world full of unseen dangers, the shepherd's presence—his rod and staff—removes fear and provides protection his sheep often don't even recognize.

context: we can say with the psalmist, and truly it is true in our lives, that my cup runneth over.
point_summary: Overflowing gratitude expressed in "my cup runneth over" is grounded not in temporal, earthly blessings but in the enduring spiritual reality that Christ continually intercedes for believers at the right hand of God.

context: we should say my cup runneth over because he's coming back for me.
point_summary: The overflowing cup of Psalm 23:5 is here grounded in Christ's promised return—a certainty that both comforts believers and motivates faithful diligence until he comes.

context: we ought to be able to say with the psalmist, my cup runneth over. My cup runneth over.
point_summary: The overflowing cup of Psalm 23:5 is not a feeling contingent on circumstances but a settled, constant reality that believers should affirm sincerely from the heart.

context: lift up our eyes unto the hills from whence cometh our help
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: knowing that our help cometh from the Lord
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: It is good for me, the psalmist says, to draw nigh unto the Lord.
context: It is good for me, the psalmist says, to draw nigh unto the Lord.
point_summary: Drawing near to God is good — a truth the psalmist affirms — framing the sermon's call to pursue ongoing fellowship with the Lord as the defining result of walking in the light.

context: 'In thy presence is fullness of joy.'
point_summary: Fullness of joy is found in God's presence alone, and walking in the light produces that fellowship with Him from which genuine, unrobbed joy flows.

context: David prayed, 'Lord, restore unto me the joy of thy salvation.'
point_summary: Sin's power to steal joy is illustrated by David's prayer in Psalm 51:12, cited here as evidence that walking in sin—not in the light—is what robs believers of the joy God intends them to have.

context: That's what we looked at on Friday evening, Psalm 103, and just our meditation of Him is sweet, and we are glad, and what joy that is.
point_summary: Meditating on who God is, as explored in Psalm 103, produces genuine joy in the believer walking in the light of His presence.

context: but thou, O Lord, art a shield for me, my glory and the lifter up of mine head.
point_summary: Before bringing his petition to God, David first rehearses God's character—declaring Him a shield, glory, and lifter of his head—as a deliberate act of reminding himself of who God is amid opposition.

context: I cried unto the Lord with my voice, and He heard me out of His holy hill, Sila.
point_summary: David's response to overwhelming opposition is distilled in a single word — prayer — as captured in the sermon's title verse: "I cried unto the Lord, and He heard me."

context: But thou, O Lord, verse number three, art a shield for me.
point_summary: Rehearsing God's identity as "a shield for me" in prayer shifts the believer's focus away from consuming trouble and toward the protecting character of God.

context: the Lord is my shepherd
point_summary: The personal pronouns of Psalm 23 ("my shepherd") are invoked as a parallel to David's "for me" in Psalm 3:3, illustrating that intimate, individual ownership of God's protection brings comfort amid adversity.

context: but Thou, O Lord, art a shield for me
point_summary: Against the enemies' taunt that God would not help him, David counters with the personal confession "Thou art a shield for me," shifting focus from the magnitude of his trouble to the character of his God.

context: He is the lifter up of mine head.
point_summary: God's role as "the lifter up of mine head" means He counters the discouragement that trials naturally produce in frail, dust-formed humanity, restoring what no circumstance can.

context: the idols of the heathen are silver and gold, the work of men's hands. They have mouths, but they speak not. Eyes have they, but they see not. They have ears, but they hear not. Neither is there any breath in their mouths.
point_summary: Idols of the heathen have ears yet cannot hear, making their inability a foil that magnifies the living God who genuinely hears His people when they pray.

context: Cast thy burden upon the Lord and He shall sustain thee. He shall never suffer the righteous to be moved.
point_summary: Casting burdens upon the Lord is the believer's response to overwhelming difficulty, with the promise that God will sustain what we cannot bear in our own strength.

context: As I think about this truth in Psalm 3 and the response of David in these verses of Scripture
point_summary: David's response in Psalm 3 is set alongside 1 Peter 5:7 and Psalm 55 to reinforce that believers must cast all care upon God because He genuinely cares for them.

context: the response of David in these verses of Scripture in Psalm 55
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And we will lift up our eyes into the hills from whence cometh our help
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: You're our glory. The lifter up of mine head
point_summary: David's shift from cataloguing his troubles to focusing on who God is to him models prayer as the one-word summary of the believer's proper response to real adversity.

context: let the words of my mouth and the meditation of my heart be acceptable in thy sight, O Lord, my strength and my Redeemer.
point_summary: Guarding both thought and speech is the unified spiritual discipline Psalm 19:14 calls for, illustrated negatively by Gehazi, whose unguarded meditation produced the lying tongue that destroyed him.

context: blessed is he whose transgression is forgiven, whose sin is covered
point_summary: Contrasted with Gehazi's persistent self-concealment, Psalm 32:1 is cited to show that true blessing belongs not to those who cover their own sin but to those whom God covers through forgiveness.

context: I acknowledged my sin unto thee and mine iniquity have I not hid
point_summary: Confession and transparency before God — not concealment — is the path to forgiveness, contrasted directly with Gehazi's refusal to acknowledge his sin before Elisha.

context: he knows our frame. He knows that we're but dust
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the psalmist said in Psalm 48 great is the Lord and greatly to be praised
point_summary: Psalm 48's declaration that "great is the Lord and greatly to be praised" is cited to reinforce the Lord's Prayer's call to hallow God's name by keeping his greatness and holiness consciously in view.

context: blessed is the man that walketh not in the counsel of the ungodly, nor standeth in the way of sinners, nor sitteth in the seat of the scornful
point_summary: Familiarity with Psalm 1 can breed spiritual autopilot; the sermon urges readers to approach even well-known Scripture freshly, asking God to reveal what they may have missed.

context: but, his delight is in the law of the Lord, and in his law doth he meditate day and night
point_summary: Familiarity with a well-known passage like Psalm 1 can breed spiritual autopilot, so readers must approach even memorized Scripture with fresh dependence on God to reveal what they may have missed.

context: And he shall be like a tree planted by the rivers of water that bringeth forth his fruit in his season. His leaf also shall not wither, and whatsoever he doeth shall prosper
point_summary: Familiarity with Psalm 1 can breed spiritual autopilot, so the sermon urges readers to approach even well-known passages with fresh dependence on God to reveal what they may have previously missed.

context: Blessed is the man that walketh not in the counsel of the ungodly, nor standeth in the way of sinners
point_summary: Psalm 1:1 traces a downward progression—walking in ungodly counsel, then standing among sinners—warning that each step deeper into worldly influence compounds the enemy's deceptive hold on the believer.

context: ...blessed is the man that walketh not in the counsel of the ungodly, nor standeth in the way of sinners...
point_summary: Psalm 1:1's three-part progression—walking, standing, sitting—illustrates how gradual exposure to ungodly influence deepens until a person is fully settled among the scornful, as Lot's trajectory toward Sodom demonstrates.

context: ...nor sitteth in the seat of the scornful.
point_summary: Psalm 1:1's three-part progression—walking, standing, sitting—illustrates how incremental exposure to ungodly influence leads to deeper entrenchment, as seen in Lot's gradual drift toward Sodom.

context: but his delight is in the law of the Lord
point_summary: Delighting in God's law, as the psalm describes, means approaching Scripture with the eager anticipation of a love letter—not as an obligation to check off.

context: We find that the first two verses of Psalm 1, we note how he acts.
point_summary: Psalm 1:1's "blessed man" is shown to be one whose delight in God's Word moves beyond morning reading into active, day-long meditation that shapes his behavior and practice.

context: We find that the first two verses of Psalm 1, we note how he acts.
point_summary: Delighting in God's Word is not merely an emotional response but a sustained, daily meditation that actively shapes thought and behavior throughout each day.

context: In verse number 3 of Psalm 1, we note how he abounds.
point_summary: The blessed man's continual meditation on God's Word produces the stability, strength, and fruitfulness pictured in the image of a tree planted by rivers of water.

context: ...whatsoever He doeth shall prosper...
point_summary: Prosperity promised in Psalm 1:3 is conditional on delighting in, meditating on, and obeying God's Word—not a blank guarantee of worldly wealth, but God's blessing on faithful obedience.

context: Psalm 37, the Bible says trust in the Lord in verse number three and do good. So shalt thou dwell in the land and verily thou shalt be fed.
point_summary: Prosperity in Psalm 37:3 is conditional—dwelling in the land and being fed follows trusting the Lord and doing good, not merely claiming the promise while ignoring its prerequisite.

context: delight thyself also in the Lord and He shall give thee the desires of thine heart.
point_summary: Delighting in the Lord is the prerequisite condition for receiving the desires of one's heart, not a promise granted apart from that prior commitment to Him.

context: ...as we look here in the passage of scripture in Psalm chapter number one...
point_summary: Psalm 1 is brought in to warn that ungodly influences—people, media, and false teaching like prosperity theology—can draw believers onto a path contrary to Scripture.

context: to get there to the blessed man in Psalm 1
point_summary: Psalm 1's "blessed man" serves as the sermon's destination, illustrating the observable actions and resulting abundance of one who guards his influences and roots himself in God's Word.

context: for the righteous Lord loveth righteousness
point_summary: Psalm 11:7's declaration that "the righteous Lord loveth righteousness" supplies the sermon's governing theme, identifying God's own righteous character as the foundation for the believer's trust in Him.

context: in the Lord put I my trust
point_summary: Psalm 11:1 opens with the psalmist's declaration of trust in Jehovah, presented as the only logical response to who God is as Lord God Almighty who rules and reigns.

context: in the Lord put I my trust, how say ye to my soul, flee as a bird to your mountain.
point_summary: Psalm 11:1's opening declaration, "In the Lord put I my trust," is presented as the psalmist's foundational confidence—the logical response to who Jehovah God is as sovereign Lord.

context: for lo, the wicked bend their bow, they make ready their arrow upon the string, that they may privily shoot at the upright in heart.
point_summary: Psalm 11:2 depicts the wicked bending their bow against the upright, illustrating that the enemy is actively on the attack against the righteous—a threat the psalmist acknowledges while already anchored in trust in the Lord.

reference_text: verse number 3, if the foundations be destroyed, what can the righteous do?
context: He says in verse number 3, if the foundations be destroyed, what can the righteous do?
point_summary: When foundations are under attack, the psalmist's question drives the eye immediately upward to God's sovereign throne as the only sufficient answer.

reference_text: verse number 4 in reminding Himself and declaring for us that the Lord reigns, it says the Lord is in His holy temple, the Lord's throne is in heaven, His eyes behold, His eyelids try the children of men.
context: Notice how His attention quickly turns in verse number 4 in reminding Himself and declaring for us that the Lord reigns, it says the Lord is in His holy temple, the Lord's throne is in heaven, His eyes behold, His eyelids try the children of men.
point_summary: Amid the wicked's assault and crumbling foundations, Psalm 11:4 redirects the psalmist's gaze upward: God remains enthroned in heaven, sovereign and reigning over every threatening circumstance.

context: the psalmist says in Psalm 145 and verse number 17, the Lord is righteous in all His ways and holy in all His works
point_summary: Psalm 145:17 is cited to establish that God's righteousness is not a developing quality but an eternal, unchanging attribute expressed in all His ways and works toward mankind.

context: ...the righteous Lord loveth righteousness...
point_summary: God's righteousness—His always doing what is just and lawful—is the basis for why righteousness exalts a nation, since a righteous God loves righteousness and extends His favor to a righteous people.

context: the wicked shall be turned into hell and all the nations that forget God
point_summary: Psalm 9:17's warning that nations which forget God are turned into hell is invoked as a biblical principle directly applicable to America's moral and spiritual departure.

context: the wicked shall be turned into hell in all the nations that forget God
point_summary: Nations that forget God face divine judgment, as Psalm 9:17 warns that the wicked — and forgetful nations — shall be turned into hell, a sobering truth the sermon applies to America without relishing its outcome.

context: Lord, open thou mine eyes that I may behold wondrous things out of thy law.
point_summary: Praying Psalm 119:18 before opening Scripture is presented as the posture of one who invites God to speak, expecting the Word to yield wondrous things to the attentive servant.

context: The earth is the Lord's and the fullness thereof, the world, and they that dwell therein.
point_summary: Psalm 24:1 is cited as scriptural confirmation that God is the owner and possessor of heaven and earth, a truth traced from Deuteronomy 10:14 through the Psalms and into Paul's New Testament application.

context: I will cry unto God Most High, unto God that performeth all things for me.
point_summary: Dependence on "God Most High" who "performeth all things" for his people equips believers, as it equipped Abram, to resist the enemy's subtle distractions and keep their eyes fixed on the Lord.

context: the psalmist declares God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble
point_summary: God is not merely capable of helping but is always available — a very present help — so that no circumstance, however overwhelming, can find Him occupied elsewhere.

context: He's a very present help in the time of trouble.
point_summary: God is not merely capable of helping but is actively available — "a very present help in trouble" — so that his people can approach him with confidence in any crisis.

context: therefore, based upon our understanding that God is our refuge and our strength and a very present help in trouble, will not we fear, though the earth be removed and though the mountains be carried into the midst of the sea,
point_summary: Because God is declared "a very present help in trouble," the psalmist's "therefore" in verse 2 grounds fearlessness amid catastrophic upheaval in that prior certainty of God's availability.

context: though the waters thereof roar and be troubled, though the mountains shake with swelling, thereof, selah.
point_summary: Because God is not merely capable of helping but is actively present and available in trouble, the believer need not fear even catastrophic upheaval—a truth the psalmist seals with *selah*, a call to stop and let it sink in.

context: God is a very present help at the time of trouble
point_summary: God's sufficiency in trouble—echoed in Psalm 46:1's declaration that He is "a very present help"—is the ground for fixing one's eyes on Him rather than on one's own strength or wisdom.

context: Nevertheless, he saved them for his namesake...notice the last phrase of Psalm 106 and verse number 8. It says this, that he might make his mighty power to be known.
point_summary: God's saving acts are not ultimately for human benefit alone but for his own namesake, that his mighty power might be made known.

context: Make a joyful noise unto the Lord, all ye lands.
point_summary: Psalm 100:1's call to "make a joyful noise unto the Lord, all ye lands" is read as part of the whole psalm's framework, with verse 4's command to enter His gates with thanksgiving singled out as the sermon's focal point.

context: Serve the Lord with gladness. Come before his presence with singing.
point_summary: Psalm 100:2's call to "serve the Lord with gladness" and "come before his presence with singing" is read as part of the psalm's full context before the sermon narrows its focus to the thanksgiving phrase of verse 4.

context: Know ye that the Lord, he is God. It is he that hath made us, and not we ourselves. We are his people, and the sheep of his pasture.
point_summary: Psalm 100:3's declaration that "it is he that hath made us, and not we ourselves" grounds the worshipper's identity as belonging to God—his people and the sheep of his pasture.

context: Enter into his gates with thanksgiving, and into his courts with praise. Be thankful unto him, and bless his name.
point_summary: Psalm 100:4's opening phrase, "Enter into his gates with thanksgiving," is introduced as the sermon's governing theme, set against the cultural problem of unthankfulness Paul identifies in 2 Timothy 3.

context: For the Lord is good, his mercy is everlasting, and his truth endureth to all generations.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: praise ye the Lord, praise God in His sanctuary. Praise Him in the firmament of His power.
point_summary: Psalm 150's repeated "praise ye the Lord" is cited as the biblical model for beginning prayer with praise and thanksgiving rather than a list of needs.

context: Praise Him for His mighty acts. Praise Him according to His excellent greatness,
point_summary: Praising God for His mighty acts and excellent greatness, as Psalm 150:2 commands, models the pattern of beginning prayer with praise and thanksgiving rather than a list of needs.

context: Praise Him with the sound of the trumpet. Praise Him with the psaltery and harp.
point_summary: Psalm 150's repeated "Praise Him" refrain, including the trumpet call of verse 3, is cited as the Old Testament model for beginning prayer with praise and thanksgiving rather than a list of needs.

context: Praise Him with the timbrel and dance. Praise Him with stringed instruments and organs.
point_summary: Psalm 150's repeated calls to praise with instruments and voice illustrate the kind of wholehearted, multifaceted thanksgiving that should characterize the believer's prayer life from the outset.

context: Praise Him upon the loud cymbals. Praise Him upon the high-sounding cymbals.
point_summary: Psalm 150's repeated calls to praise with every instrument serve as the Old Testament model for beginning prayer with thanksgiving and praise rather than a list of needs.

context: Everything that hath breath, praise the Lord. Praise ye the Lord.
point_summary: Psalm 150:6's universal call for everything with breath to praise the Lord grounds the sermon's point that thanksgiving and praise should be the starting posture of every believer's prayer life.

context: I'd like to just share some thoughts with you this evening with regard to enter into His gates with thanksgiving. From this thought in Psalm 100, in verse number 4,
point_summary: Entering God's presence with thanksgiving, as commanded in Psalm 100:4, is grounded in a pattern of praise drawn from Psalm 150 and reinforced by New Testament calls to thankfulness in Ephesians, Philippians, and 1 Thessalonians.

context: praise Him for His mighty acts, praise Him according to His excellent greatness
point_summary: Psalm 150:2's twofold structure — praising God for His mighty acts and His excellent greatness — is taken in reverse order as the organizing framework for what believers ought to thank God for.

context: Pour out your heart before Him. And the psalmist said He is a refuge for us.
point_summary: Psalm 62's declaration that God is a refuge grounds thanksgiving for His sheltering, dependable presence, to whom believers are called to pour out their hearts.

context: He is a very present help in a time of trouble.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I will lift up mine eyes unto the hills from whence cometh my help.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: My help cometh from the Lord who made heaven and earth.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: We look at Psalm 150 in verse number two. It says at the end of the verse, praise him according to his excellent greatness.
point_summary: Praising God "according to his excellent greatness" calls believers not merely to acknowledge God's attributes intellectually, but to actively thank him for each one in prayer.

context: The Psalmist says in Psalm 135, I know that the Lord is great.
point_summary: Knowing that the Lord is great is not enough — the sermon presses whether believers actually thank God for that greatness in their daily prayer lives.

context: the psalmist said in Psalm 23, the Lord is my shepherd
point_summary: Psalm 23:1 grounds the point that God, as shepherd, actively provides for his people — a reason for thanksgiving when entering prayer.

context: teach me thy way, O Lord. I will walk in thy truth.
point_summary: Psalm 86:11's pairing of "teach me thy way" with "I will walk in thy truth" is cited to show that receiving God's instruction carries an obligation to obey it, not merely hear it.

context: Who by the mouth of thy servant David has said, why did the heathen rage? And the people imagined vain things.
point_summary: The early church's prayer cites Psalm 2:1 to interpret the rulers' opposition to Jesus as the fulfillment of David's prophetic word about nations and kings raging against the Lord and His anointed.

context: The kings of the earth stood up and the rulers were gathered together against the Lord and against his Christ.
point_summary: The early church's prayer identifies the gathering of Herod, Pilate, Gentiles, and Israel against Jesus as the direct fulfillment of the rulers conspiring against the Lord and His Christ in Psalm 2:2.

context: reference to Psalm 2, with regard to the heathen raging and the kings of the earth standing against the Lord
point_summary: Psalm 2's prophecy of raging nations and kings opposing the Lord is cited by the early church as Old Testament confirmation that Christ's crucifixion was divinely determined, not a defeat.

context: 'Praise ye the Lord! Praise the Lord, O my soul! While I live will I praise the Lord. I will sing praises unto my God while I have any being. Put not your trust in princes, nor in the Son of Man, in whom there is no help.'
point_summary: Psalm 146:1's call to praise the Lord with one's whole soul, set against the warning not to trust in princes, frames the Creator God as the only reliable helper for those facing opposition.

context: 'Put not your trust in princes, nor in the Son of Man, in whom there is no help.'
point_summary: Psalm 146:3 warns against misplacing trust in human leaders or self-reliance, since mortal help is no help at all, contrasting the frailty of man with the Creator God who is the believer's true helper.

context: 'His breath goeth forth, he returneth to his earth, and that very day his thoughts perish.'
point_summary: The fleeting mortality of human leaders—their breath departing, their thoughts perishing—contrasts with the enduring help of the Creator God, underscoring why trust must rest in Him alone.

context: 'Happy is he that hath the God of Jacob for his help, whose hope is in the Lord his God.'
point_summary: The blessedness of trusting God rather than human princes rests on His identity as the God of Jacob—a personal, covenant helper—in contrast to mortal men whose help perishes with their last breath.

context: blessed is the one that has as his help the God of Jacob for his help, whose hope is in the Lord his God.
point_summary: Genuine happiness and blessing belong to those whose help and hope rest in the God of Jacob — the Creator of heaven, earth, and sea — the same God the disciples invoked in Acts 4.

context: Which made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that therein is.
point_summary: Psalm 146:6's description of God as the one "which made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that therein is" directly echoes the disciples' prayer in Acts 4, identifying the Creator God as the helper in whom believers are blessed to trust.

context: The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want. He maketh me to lie down in green pastures.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: He leadeth me beside the still waters.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: He restoreth my soul. He leadeth me in the paths of righteousness for his namesake.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil for thou art with me. Thy rod and thy staff, they comfort me.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Thou preparest a table before me in the presence of mine enemies. Thou anointest my head with oil, my cup runneth over.
point_summary: Psalm 23's opening declaration "the Lord is my shepherd" establishes a personal, possessive relationship with God that frames every provision described throughout the Psalm, including the overflowing cup.

context: Surely goodness and mercy shall follow me all the days of my life, and I will dwell in the house of the Lord forever.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: The truth of verse number one, the Lord is my shepherd is the basis for the truth in verse number six
point_summary: The confession "the Lord is my shepherd" in verse 1 is presented as the necessary foundation for the assurance of verse 6—one cannot claim to dwell in the Lord's house forever without knowing Him as shepherd.

context: the truth in verse number six, that I will dwell in the house of the Lord forever
point_summary: The eternal security of dwelling in the Lord's house forever rests entirely on the foundational truth of verse one — that the Lord is one's shepherd — the two verses forming the psalm's defining bookends.

context: my cup runneth over
point_summary: The phrase "my cup runneth over" reveals the abundance of God's goodness to His children, imagery that surpasses the half-full/half-empty measure of mere optimism.

context: the Lord hath daily, He's daily loadeth me with benefits
point_summary: Daily divine loading with benefits—drawn from Psalm 68:19—grounds the sermon's claim that God's blessing is not merely sprinkled but abundantly heaped upon His children.

context: my cup runneth over
point_summary: The overflowing cup of Psalm 23:5 is read as a declaration of God's abundant goodness to His children, surpassing the optimist-pessimist "half full" metaphor entirely.

context: my cup runneth over
point_summary: Psalm 23:5's overflowing cup reframes the optimist-pessimist metaphor entirely: the believer's cup is not half full or half empty but running over, giving ten biblical reasons for thanksgiving.

context: I shall dwell in the house of the Lord forever.
point_summary: The eternal dwelling promised in Psalm 23:6 is grounded in the shepherd's personal pursuit—because the Lord found and claimed his sheep, they are assured of his house forever.

context: my cup, surely, it runs over. My cup runneth over.
point_summary: God's care for his people is not a feeling or circumstance but a declared truth of Scripture, grounded in 1 Peter 5:7, that makes the overflowing cup of Psalm 23:5 a matter of perspective.

context: In Psalm 55, in verse number 22, the Bible says, cast thy burden upon the Lord. Casting all your care upon him, we read in 1 Peter 5, 7, why? For he careth for you. Here we find in Psalm 55 and verse 22, cast thy burden upon the Lord and he shall sustain thee. He shall never suffer the righteous to be moved.
point_summary: Casting every burden upon the Lord carries the promise not merely that he *can* sustain, but that he *shall* sustain—a certainty the text's language demands.

context: cast thy burden upon the Lord and he shall sustain thee
point_summary: The certainty of God's sustaining help rests on the declarative "he shall," not merely "he can" or "he might," making the promise unconditional for those who cast their burdens upon Him.

context: And here in Psalm 23 and verse number 1, the Bible says, the Lord is my shepherd. Notice the next words, I shall not want.
point_summary: Because the Lord is the shepherd who provides everything his sheep need, the declaration "I shall not want" means his people lack nothing.

context: The Bible says in verse number 2 of Psalm 23, he maketh me to lie down in green pastures.
point_summary: God's provision through the shepherd is pictured in "green pastures" and "still waters" as the specific nourishment and calm supply that sheep—and believers—require and cannot secure for themselves.

context: he leadeth me beside what? The still waters.
point_summary: God's provision of green pastures and still waters illustrates that a good shepherd supplies exactly what his sheep need, pointing toward mankind's greatest need: salvation and reconciliation to God.

context: we should say my cup runneth over. Because my greatest need has been met.
point_summary: Psalm 23:5's overflowing cup is interpreted as the believer's assurance that their greatest need—salvation and reconciliation to God—has already been fully met by the Shepherd.

context: I don't know that the Lord is my shepherd personally.
point_summary: Psalm 23:1's declaration that the LORD is "my shepherd" grounds the claim that humanity's greatest need—salvation and reconciliation to God—is precisely what this Shepherd knows and moves to supply.

context: we're told to number our days
point_summary: Numbering our days matters because death's arrival is unknown to every person, making readiness—not age or failing health—the only fitting response to life's brevity.

context: Psalm 51 is a great passage of Scripture that deals with the psalm of repentance of David.
point_summary: Psalm 51, David's psalm of repentance, illustrates that the desire holiness requires is not merely to be *cleaner* but to be *clean*—a distinction the sermon ties directly to approaching God with undefiled hands in prayer.

context: if I regard iniquity in my heart, the Lord will not hear me
point_summary: Cherished sin — iniquity deliberately retained in the heart — does not merely impede God's hearing but forfeits it entirely, since the text says He *will not* hear, not that He *cannot*.

context: we say, as David said, wash me, cleanse me
point_summary: Unconfessed sin separates the believer from a God who is fully able to hear and save, making cleansing—as David sought in Psalm 51—a prerequisite for effectual prayer.

context: against thee, and the only. Have I sinned and done this wickedness
point_summary: Sin separating the believer from God's hearing ear must be confessed and cleansed—as David modeled in crying out for washing—before prayer can be effectual.

context: Great peace have they which love thy law, and nothing shall offend them.
point_summary: Those who love God's law have great peace and cannot be offended, illustrating that Spirit-controlled surrender to Christ removes the wrath that hinders effectual prayer.

context: Psalm chapter three, we're going to start there
point_summary: Psalm 3:4 (cited as Psalm 3:1 in the reference) is brought forward as direct scriptural evidence that God hears the prayers of His people, grounding the call to pray with confidence rather than doubt.

context: I cried unto the Lord with my voice and He heard me out of His holy hill, Sila.
point_summary: Psalm 3:4 is cited as scriptural assurance that God hears the prayers of those who cry out to Him, grounding the call to pray with confidence rather than doubt.

context: the Bible speaks about those false idols in verse number six and says, they have ears, but they hear not.
point_summary: Psalm 115:6's description of deaf idols that "hear not" stands in deliberate contrast to the living God of Psalm 116, who inclines His ear and hears prayer, grounding the believer's confidence to call upon Him.

context: I love the Lord because he hath heard my voice and my supplications
point_summary: The psalmist's declaration of love for God is grounded specifically in the confidence that God hears prayer, making answered prayer the very motivation for lifelong, persistent calling upon Him.

context: In my distress, have you ever been there?
point_summary: Psalm 120:1 is cited as evidence that God hears prayer even in distress, grounding the call to pray with confidence rather than doubt or hesitation.

context: The psalmist says in Psalm 119, it's time, Lord, for thee to work.
point_summary: Psalm 119's cry "It is time, Lord, for thee to work" is invoked as a timeless plea for divine intervention, made even more urgent for believers today.

context: David says, I was glad when they said unto me, let us go into the house of the Lord.
point_summary: David's gladness in Psalm 122:1, drawn from the Hebrew word meaning to exult and be gleesome, models the expectant, joyful posture believers should bring when gathering for corporate worship.

context: David said, listen, I was glad when they said unto me, let us go into the house of the Lord.
point_summary: David's word for "glad" (Hebrew *tzalmach*) carries the sense of merrymaking, exultation, and cheerfulness, pointing to the expectation with which God's people ought to approach corporate worship.

context: I can see it in Psalm 51 after that thing that he did with Bathsheba and God broke him and he got right with the Lord, amen.
point_summary: Psalm 51 is cited as evidence that David, unlike Solomon, made the necessary course correction after his sin with Bathsheba—confessing, repenting, and being restored to fellowship with God.

context: The psalmist cries out in Psalm 119, oh how love I thy law, it is my meditation all the day long.
point_summary: Genuine love for God's Word is measured by the soul's reluctance to leave it, as the psalmist's cry—"Oh how love I thy law"—illustrates a meditation-driven devotion that sanctification requires.

context: I will declare the decree, the Lord hath said unto me, thou art my son, this day have I begotten thee.
point_summary: Psalm 2:7's declaration "thou art my son, this day have I begotten thee" is interpreted here not as a reference to the incarnation but to the resurrection, confirmed by its citation in Acts 13.

reference_text: second Psalm, thou art my son, this day have I begotten thee
context: as it is also written in the second Psalm, thou art my son, this day have I begotten thee
point_summary: Paul's citation of Psalm 2:7 in Acts 13 interprets "this day have I begotten thee" as fulfilled not in the incarnation but in the resurrection of Jesus.

context: open down mine eyes that I may behold wondrous things out of thy law
point_summary: Psalm 119:18's prayer for opened eyes illustrates the divine illumination required to understand Scripture, paralleling Luke 24:45 where the risen Christ opened the disciples' understanding.

context: The entrance of thy words giveth light
point_summary: The entrance of God's Word giving light (Ps. 119:130) is paired with Psalm 119:18 to illustrate the divine illumination by which Christ opened the disciples' understanding of Scripture in Luke 24:45.

context: We find, if you take your Bible, turn to the book of Psalms, if you will, Psalm 19, we're gonna look at a number of verses of Scripture in order to prove this point with regard to the prescription and the process.
point_summary: God's word is the authoritative instrument for warning the unruly, and Psalm 19 is introduced as scriptural proof of both the prescription and the process for delivering that warning.

context: Moreover, by them, speaking of the law of the Lord, the statutes of the Lord, the judgments of the Lord
point_summary: God's word itself is the instrument of warning for the unruly, as Psalm 19:11 declares that the servant is warned by the law, statutes, and judgments of the Lord.

context: I will lift up mine eyes into the hills from whence cometh my help.
point_summary: Psalm 121:1–2 is cited as a familiar refrain reinforcing the sermon's central theme that God is the sole source of help when his people face impossible circumstances.

context: My help cometh from the Lord, which made heaven and earth.
point_summary: God's help comes from the Lord who made heaven and earth, cited here as scriptural grounding for the sermon's recurring theme that believers can confidently call on an all-powerful God in times of trouble.

context: God is our refuge, our strength and a very present help in the time of trouble.
point_summary: God's identity as refuge, strength, and ever-present help in trouble is cited from a prior Thursday study to anchor the sermon's broader theme that calling on such a God in crisis brings deliverance.

context: the passage of Scripture in that familiar Psalm, Psalm 23, and we dealt with the fact that the Lord is my shepherd
point_summary: Psalm 23's declaration that the Lord is shepherd grounds the broader truth, referenced here in passing, that God provides for and protects his people as a shepherd cares for sheep.

context: Psalm 119 and verse 18, we're familiar with the verse of Scripture.
point_summary: Approaching Scripture with desire and expectation to hear from God is itself a prayer, as the psalmist models by crying out for opened eyes to behold wondrous things from God's law.

context: 'Open thou mine eyes, that I may behold wondrous things out of thy law.'
point_summary: Approaching Scripture with desire and expectation to hear from God—modeled by the psalmist's cry for opened eyes—is presented as the necessary posture for genuine encounter with God's Word.

context: I think about that verse that I quoted in Psalm 118.
point_summary: Scripture itself models the posture of expectant, dependent listening, as the psalmist's cry to have his eyes opened frames the preacher's own daily prayer before reading God's Word.

context: I pray those two thoughts from Scripture, Psalm 119 and verse 18
point_summary: Praying Psalm 119:18 daily as a personal petition reflects the conviction that Scripture's wonders are divinely unveiled only to those who approach it with expectant, dependent hearts.

context: In Psalm 42, verses one and two, the Bible says, as the heart panteth after the water brooks, so panteth my soul after thee, O God.
point_summary: Psalm 42:1's image of the deer panting for water illustrates the soul's deep, urgent longing to hear from God—a thirst satisfied when one comes expectantly to Scripture.

context: In Psalm 42, verses one and two, the Bible says, as the heart panteth after the water brooks, so panteth my soul after thee, O God. Notice it says, my soul thirsteth for God, for the living God.
point_summary: Psalm 42:2's image of the soul thirsting for God illustrates the deep, urgent longing to hear from God that believers bring when they open Scripture.

context: Psalm 96 and verse number 8
point_summary: Psalm 96:8's command to "give unto the Lord the glory due unto his name" is cited as a foundational Old Testament expression of the biblical principle that praise and thanksgiving to God are expected and appropriate.

context: Psalm 147 in verse number one
point_summary: Psalm 147:1 is cited to reinforce the biblical principle that praising God is not merely commanded but declared fitting and pleasant, as the psalmist's own language affirms its appropriateness.

context: praise ye the Lord notice For it is good to sing praises unto our God
point_summary: Praising God is declared not merely commanded — the psalmist frames it as inherently good, pleasant, and beautiful, making thanksgiving a fitting and comely act for God's people.

context: I will give thanks in the great congregation. I will praise Thee among much people.
point_summary: Praising God publicly in a great congregation magnifies Him before many witnesses and creates opportunity to testify of what He has done.

context: I will praise Thee forever because Thou hast done it. And I will wait on Thy name for it is good before Thy saints.
point_summary: Praising God "forever" in Psalm 52:9 grounds an eternal dimension of thanksgiving—extending the believer's earthly resolve to praise through all of life into unending worship in eternity.

context: Psalm 54 and verse number 6, I will freely sacrifice unto Thee. I will praise Thy name, O Lord, for it is good.
point_summary: Freely sacrificing and praising God's name is presented as a brief marker within a chain of psalms establishing the believer's lifelong, public, and eternal commitment to praise.

context: Psalm 56 and verse number 4, In God I will praise His word. In God I have put my trust. I will not fear what flesh can do unto me.
point_summary: Trusting God's word banishes fear of what flesh can do, grounding praise not in circumstances but in confident reliance on Him.

context: Psalm 57 and verse number 9, I will praise Thee, O Lord, among the people. I will sing unto Thee among the nations.
point_summary: Public praise carries unique impact—declaring God's goodness among the people both exalts Him before others and encourages fellow believers, making corporate testimony essential alongside private worship.

context: as we mentioned earlier in Psalm 35 and verse number 18
point_summary: Praising God publicly among many people both magnifies the Savior and creates opportunity to testify of His works, making it the appropriate—if not always popular—response.

context: In Psalm 9 and verse number 1, he said with my whole heart.
point_summary: Wholehearted praise—the same phrase the psalmist uses in Psalm 9:1—is held up as the standard for passionate, undivided worship of God, contrasted with the fervor people readily show for sports.

context: I will praise the Lord among the people and I will sing praises unto thee among the nations.
point_summary: Praising God publicly—among the peoples and nations—is presented as one of many psalmist declarations showing that wholehearted, expressive worship is both biblical and appropriate.

context: I will greatly praise the Lord with my mouth. Yea, I will praise Him among the multitude.
point_summary: Wholehearted, vocal praise offered publicly among the multitude is presented as the psalmist's personal, deliberate commitment, cited alongside parallel texts to urge passionate congregational worship.

context: praise you the Lord. And then it says this, I will praise the Lord with my whole heart in the assembly of the upright and in the congregation.
point_summary: Wholehearted, congregational praise—voiced publicly "in the assembly of the upright and in the congregation"—is presented as the biblical pattern for passionate, uninhibited worship of God.

context: open to me the gates of righteousness. I will go into them and I will praise the Lord.
point_summary: Entering the gates of righteousness leads directly to praising the Lord, cited here as one of many psalmic declarations of wholehearted, intentional praise.

context: I will praise thee for thou hast heard me and art become my salvation.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: thou art my God, I will praise thee. Thou art my God, I will exalt thee.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I will praise thee with uprightness of heart
point_summary: Wholehearted praise is rightly grounded in having learned God's righteous judgments, linking gratitude to knowledge of who He is and what He has done.

context: I will praise thee with my whole heart before the gods will I sing praise unto thee.
point_summary: Psalm 138:1 is cited as one of many "I will" declarations across the Psalms, illustrating that praise is a purposeful, resolved commitment of the whole heart rather than a passive response.

context: I will praise thee for I am fearfully and wonderfully made. Marvelous are thy works and that my soul knoweth right well.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Every day will I bless thee and I will praise thy name forever and ever.
point_summary: Psalm 145:2 is cited as the culminating example in a chain of "I will" praise declarations, showing that genuine thanksgiving involves a purposeful, daily resolve to bless and praise God's name.

context: Will thou not revive thy people again?
point_summary: Revival is the church's urgent need and genuine possibility, invoked through the plea of Psalm 85:6 against the warning of Laodicean lukewarmness.

reference_text: The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want
context: The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want. He leads us, He provides for us.
point_summary: God's provision for Elijah—supernatural food sustaining him forty days—is briefly illuminated by Psalm 23:1 as evidence that the Lord-Shepherd knows and supplies what His sheep need, even beyond natural expectation.

context: The book of Psalms, if you will, this morning, Psalm 34. We'll be looking at the 34th Psalm, and we'll be looking at several verses within the psalm.
point_summary: What we believe about God shapes how we behave, and how we behave reveals what we truly believe—a principle the sermon will trace through Psalm 34.

context: As we look at Psalm 34, there's a phrase that I've used for our title this morning, this poor man cried.
point_summary: David's belief about God directly shaped his prayer life, illustrated by the cry of "this poor man" as a model of prayer that expects God alone to act and receive glory.

context: I will bless the Lord at all times. His praise shall continually be in my mouth.
point_summary: David's unwavering praise "at all times" in verse 1 grounds the sermon's central thesis: what a believer holds to be true about God directly shapes and drives his prayer life.

context: My soul shall make her boast in the Lord. The humble shall hear thereof and be glad.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Oh, magnify the Lord with me and let us exalt his name together.
point_summary: David's belief about God directly shapes his prayer life, as seen in the call of Psalm 34:3 to magnify and exalt the Lord together in corporate worship and trust.

context: I sought the Lord and he heard me and delivered me from all my fears.
point_summary: David's belief about God directly shaped his prayer life, as seen in verse 4's testimony that seeking the Lord brought deliverance from all his fears.

context: They looked unto him and were lightened and their faces were not ashamed.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: This poor man cried and the Lord heard him and saved him out of all his troubles.
point_summary: David's belief about God directly shaped his prayer life, as seen in this poor man's cry that moved God to hear and save him out of all his troubles.

context: The angel of the Lord encampeth round about them that fear him and delivereth them.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Oh, taste and see that the Lord is good. Blessed is the man that trusteth in him.
point_summary: Tasting and seeing that the Lord is good—affirmed through David's own experience—grounds the call to trust God, whose goodness remains certain regardless of circumstances.

context: oh, magnify the Lord with me and let us exalt his name together.
point_summary: David's call in Psalm 34:3 to "magnify the Lord" together is presented as an invitation to corporate worship, distinct from yet complementary to private devotion, where the gathered saints jointly exalt God's name.

context: I sought the Lord, and he heard me
point_summary: David's seeking the Lord in verse 4 is identified as prayer, evidenced by the Lord's response of hearing—paralleling verse 6's "this poor man cried."

context: This poor man cried, and the Lord heard him
point_summary: David's description of "this poor man" crying and being heard establishes that both verse 4 and verse 6 refer to prayer, grounded in the repeated testimony that the Lord heard him.

context: I sought the Lord and He heard me, and delivered me from all my fears.
point_summary: David's cry to God in Psalm 34:4 was rooted not merely in desperation but in his knowledge of God's character, omniscience, and omnipotence—theology that both motivates and measures genuine prayer.

context: I sought the Lord and the Lord heard me and he delivered me, what, from all my fears.
point_summary: God's peace—unlike the world's definition of peace as mere absence of conflict—delivers believers from fear, connecting David's deliverance from fears in Psalm 34:4 to Christ's promise of peace in John 14:27.

context: David said I sought the Lord and He heard me and He delivered me from all my fears.
point_summary: God's peace, likened to an umpire whose authority is final, is the answer David models in Psalm 34:4—seeking the Lord who hears and delivers from all fears.

context: I sought the Lord and He heard me and delivered me from all my fears.
point_summary: Seeking the Lord in fear finds its answer in God's deliverance, because David's confidence to cry out rested on his knowledge of who God is.

context: This poor man cried and the Lord heard him and saved him out of all his troubles.
point_summary: Psalm 34:6 is set alongside verse 4 to show that the same God who delivers from fear also hears the cry of the troubled and saves them out of all their troubles.

context: As we examine the life of David in these first several verses of Psalm 34, the amazing thing to me and the application that I want us to walk away with this morning is that David is sharing in the beginning of this portion of Scripture of Psalm 34 his personal experience.
point_summary: Psalm 34:1 opens David's personal testimony of crying to God and being delivered, illustrating that genuine prayer flows from a lived experience of God's omnipotent faithfulness.

context: Psalm 105 this morning, and be looking at verses 23 down through verse number 28.
point_summary: Israel's sojourn in Egypt, where God would display His greatness through Moses' obedience before Pharaoh, sets the stage for the plagues that follow.

context: Israel also came into Egypt and Jacob sojourned in the land of Ham
point_summary: Israel's sojourn and growth in Egypt, as recounted in Psalm 105:23, sets the historical stage for God's subsequent display of greatness through signs and wonders against his people's enemies.

context: and he increased his people greatly and made them stronger than their enemies
point_summary: God's dramatic increase of Israel's population in Egypt is presented as a sovereign act of divine power that set the stage for his signs and wonders among their enemies.

context: He turned their heart to hate his people to deal subtly with his servants
point_summary: God's sovereign orchestration of Egyptian hostility toward Israel — turning their hearts to hate his people — is presented as the providential setup through which his signs and wonders would be displayed.

context: He sent Moses his servant Aaron whom he had chosen
point_summary: God's sovereign initiative in redemption is highlighted by the psalmist's identification of Moses and Aaron as chosen servants whom He sent to display His signs in Egypt.

context: They showed his signs among them and wonders in the land of Ham
point_summary: The "wonders" of Psalm 105:27 are identified as miracles—special displays of God's power—fulfilling his promise in Exodus 7:3 to multiply signs and wonders in Egypt.

context: he sent darkness and made it dark. Now understand that these are not in the same order that the plagues came as you read Exodus seven through 11, but it says here, he sent darkness and made it dark and they rebelled not against his word.
point_summary: Psalm 105:28's placement of the plague of darkness first in the psalmist's list introduces God's sovereign display of omnipotence over Egypt, framed here as evidence that nothing is too hard for the Lord.

context: they forgot God their Savior, which had done great things in Egypt
point_summary: Israel's forgetting of God their Savior despite His great works in Egypt illustrates the sermon's warning that repeated divine displays of power did not secure lasting remembrance among His people.

context: He made His greatness known in Egypt
point_summary: God's display of wonders in Egypt was intended to imprint His power on Israel's memory, yet Psalm 106:22 indicts them for forgetting the very greatness He had made known there.

context: our righteous God loveth righteousness. I'm thankful that our God is righteous. He's not becoming righteous. He is righteous and he loveth righteousness and we read that in Psalm 11 in verse number seven.
point_summary: God's righteousness is not an evolving attribute but an eternal, unchanging reality — he loveth righteousness — cited as the divine standard against which unrighteous decrees and legislated wickedness are measured.

context: I want to look at Psalm 103 and I understand that even As we think about the holiday
point_summary: Psalm 103 is introduced as a Memorial Day text to frame the call for believers to remember their spiritual heritage, just as national memorials preserve the memory of those who sacrificed for freedom.

context: The Bible says bless the Lord. Oh my soul and All that is within me bless his holy name
point_summary: David's whole-being summons — "all that is within me" — frames Psalm 103:1 not as rote devotion but as passionate, total-soul blessing rooted in personal experience of God's goodness.

context: David said in Psalm 34 taste and see that the Lord is good
point_summary: Tasting God's goodness, as David declares in Psalm 34:8, grounds the passionate, whole-souled blessing of Psalm 103:1 and guards against the forgetfulness that plagued Israel.

context: Bless the Lord. Oh my soul notice verse 2 and forget not all His benefits
point_summary: Forgetting God's benefits is a real and recurring danger, illustrated by Israel's history, making David's command to "forget not" an urgent, active discipline for God's people.

context: blessed be the Lord, who daily loadeth us with benefits, even the God of our salvation, Selah.

context: my God daily loadeth me with benefits
point_summary: God's daily loading of benefits is not scarce or reserved but abundant and unceasing, demanding a deliberate pause—a "Selah"—to meditate on that reality.

context: take and turn to Psalm 103
point_summary: Bless the Lord, O my soul — the psalmist's self-directed command calls believers to actively rehearse God's daily, abundant benefits rather than allowing forgetfulness to diminish gratitude.

context: bless the Lord, O my soul, and all that is within me, bless his holy name
point_summary: Rehearsing God's benefits—speaking them aloud and returning to them repeatedly—is the practical means by which the soul obeys the command to "forget not all his benefits" in Psalm 103:1–2.

context: bless the Lord, O my soul, and forget not all his benefits
point_summary: Forgetting God's benefits is countered by actively rehearsing them—speaking them aloud and returning to them repeatedly—so that what is recognized does not slip from memory.

context: He hath not dealt with us after our sins, nor rewarded us according to our iniquities.
point_summary: God's mercy in not dealing with us according to our sins is cited as the scriptural ground for the benefit of forgiveness—both the one-time redemption and the daily cleansing available to believers.

reference_text: For as the heaven is high above the earth, so great is His mercy toward them that fear Him.
context: For as the heaven is high above the earth, so great is His mercy toward them that fear Him.
point_summary: God's mercy toward those who fear Him is so vast — as high as heaven above earth — that He removes transgressions completely, grounding daily forgiveness in His immeasurable lovingkindness.

reference_text: As far as the east is from the west, so far hath He removed our transgressions from us.
context: As far as the east is from the west, so far hath He removed our transgressions from us.
point_summary: God's removal of transgressions "as far as the east is from the west" (v. 12) illustrates the completeness of divine forgiveness, encompassing both the one-time redemption at salvation and the daily cleansing that restores fellowship with God.

context: who satisfieth thy mouth with good things, so that thy youth is renewed like the eagles
point_summary: God's provision—satisfying his people with good things and renewing their strength—is cited as a call to remember and rehearse his benefits, illustrated by Israel's manna and Jesus's teaching in Matthew 6.

context: The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want
point_summary: God's provision satisfies every want, illustrated by the shepherd's complete care in Psalm 23:1, set alongside Israel's ingratitude despite miraculous daily supply.

context: the nation of Israel many times throughout the Psalms in particular, you'll find where the Bible talks about how He was saved from His enemies.
point_summary: God's provision—satisfying His people with good things—is numbered among the benefits the psalmist commands us not to forget, encompassing food, shelter, and every material need He graciously supplies.

context: He made known His ways unto Moses, His acts unto the children of Israel.
point_summary: God's revelation of His ways to Moses illustrates that Scripture itself is His provision of direction for daily living, showing His people how to please and approach a holy God.

reference_text: Thy Word is a lamp unto my feet, a light to my path
context: Thy Word is a lamp unto my feet, a light to my path. The Bible provides direction for daily living.
point_summary: God's Word, cited from Psalm 119:105, is presented as the divine direction manual that guides believers in how to live and how to please and approach a holy God.

context: The Lord is merciful and gracious, slow to anger and plenteous in mercy.

context: Like as a father pitieth his children, so the Lord pitieth them that fear Him.
point_summary: God's compassion toward His people is likened to a father's deep, tender affection—the repeated word "pitieth" carrying the meaning of loving deeply and being moved with compassion toward those who fear Him.

context: For He knoweth our frame, He remembereth that we are dust.

context: As for man, his days are as grass, as a flower the field, so he flourisheth.

context: For the wind passeth over it and it is gone. And the place thereof shall know it no more.

context: For he knoweth our frame; he remembereth that we are dust.
point_summary: God's knowledge of human frailty — that He formed us and remembers we are dust — grounds His compassionate, gracious response to our weakness and need.

context: Bless the Lord, O my soul, in all that is within me. Bless His holy name.

context: Bless the Lord, O my soul, and forget not all His benefits.
point_summary: Forgetting God's benefits is countered by the deliberate act of rehearsing them one by one, as the psalmist's command to "forget not" implies an active, ongoing discipline of remembrance.

context: I kind of coupled several together in verse number eight, when I spoke about who God is.

context: The Bible speaks of, in Psalm 116 and verse 15, precious in

context: In the courts of the Lord's house, in the midst of thee, O Jerusalem, praise ye the Lord.

context: O praise the Lord, all ye nations, praise Him, all ye people,
point_summary: Psalm 117's declaration that "the truth of the Lord endureth forever" is set against contemporary relativism as an affirmation that divine truth is absolute, not subjective.

point_summary: Rehearsing God's benefits daily produces a response of praise, thanksgiving, and surrender, as the psalmist's call to "forget not" moves from recognition to lived worship.

context: The Bible talks about here in Psalm 106 the way in which they talked and spoke badly about the Lord and how they were doing that even within their tents.

context: They forgot God their Savior which had done great things in Egypt.

context: the book of Psalms this evening, Psalm 78. And I'll be looking here at the first eight verses, Psalm 78, verses one through eight.

context: the book of Psalms this evening, Psalm 78. And I'll be looking here at the first eight verses, Psalm 78, verses one through eight.

context: the book of Psalms this evening, Psalm 78. And I'll be looking here at the first eight verses, Psalm 78, verses one through eight.

context: the book of Psalms this evening, Psalm 78. And I'll be looking here at the first eight verses, Psalm 78, verses one through eight.
point_summary: Faithful transmission of God's praises across generations requires intentional refusal to hide them, ensuring the generation to come receives what fathers have declared.

context: the book of Psalms this evening, Psalm 78. And I'll be looking here at the first eight verses, Psalm 78, verses one through eight.

context: the book of Psalms this evening, Psalm 78. And I'll be looking here at the first eight verses, Psalm 78, verses one through eight.

context: Give ear, O my people, to my law: incline your ears to the words of my mouth.

context: I will open my mouth in a parable: I will utter dark sayings of old:
point_summary: The psalmist's declaration in verse 2 that he will "utter dark sayings of old" frames the entire psalm as a deliberate, structured transmission of inherited truth to future generations.

context: Which we have heard and known, and our fathers have told us.

context: We will not hide them from their children, shewing to the generation to come the praises of the LORD, and his strength, and his wonderful works that he hath done.
