point_summary: Psalm 119's pervasive emphasis on keeping, obeying, doing, and walking in God's law is cited as scriptural grounding for Ezra's defining characteristic: being a doer of the Word, not a hearer only.

context: The Bible says in Psalm 116, in verse number 15, precious in the sight of the Lord is the death of his saints.
point_summary: The death of a saint like Joseph is not a tragedy but something God himself regards as precious, framing the close of Joseph's life within divine care rather than mere mortality.

context: this poor man cried and the Lord heard him and saved him out of all his troubles
point_summary: David's personal testimony in Psalm 34:6 is presented as evidence that God hears and delivers the troubled who cry out, whether their afflictions are self-inflicted or entirely outside their control.

context: look at verse 19 if you will
point_summary: Even the righteous face many afflictions, but God's pattern—seen in David's testimony and Joseph's life—is deliverance out of them all.

context: I'm actually going to back it up and look at verse 17
point_summary: God's pattern of hearing and delivering the righteous from trouble is grounded in Psalm 34:17 as a universal promise extending beyond David's personal testimony to all who cry out.

context: The righteous cry, and the Lord heareth and delivereth them out of all their troubles.
point_summary: God hears and delivers the righteous out of all their troubles, a promise the sermon grounds in David's personal testimony before applying it to Joseph's faithfulness amid prolonged suffering.

context: The Lord is nigh unto them that are of a broken heart, and save it such as be of a contrite spirit.
point_summary: God's nearness to the brokenhearted is cited alongside surrounding verses to affirm that the righteous, though afflicted with many troubles, have a God who delivers them and works purpose through difficulty.

context: Many are the afflictions of the righteous, but the Lord delivereth him out of them all.
point_summary: Many are the afflictions of the righteous, yet God's deliverance is certain and complete — a promise grounding Joseph's prolonged suffering in prison within a broader pattern of divine faithfulness to the righteous.

context: Why would the psalmist stand up and say, great is the Lord and greatly to be praised?
point_summary: The greatness of God declared in Psalm 145:3 served as the organizing theme for an entire year of preaching, moving from discovering why God is great to declaring that greatness to a world that does not know him.

context: Wherewithal shall a young man cleanse his way, by taking heed thereto according to thy word.
point_summary: Memorizing Scripture is presented as a neglected but vital discipline, with Psalm 119:9 and 11 together grounding the call to hide God's Word in the heart as a safeguard against sin.

context: Thy word have I hid in mine heart, that I might not sin against thee.
point_summary: Hiding God's Word in the heart through memorization is presented as a safeguard against sin and a vital discipline the church has largely abandoned to children alone.

context: thou hast put gladness in my heart more than in the time that their corn and their wine increased
point_summary: The gladness God places in the believer's heart surpasses even the joy of material abundance, illustrating that knowing God's love is the supreme cause for rejoicing.

context: Read in the book of Psalms, Psalm 119. The longest chapter in all of the Bible.
point_summary: Psalm 119's psalmist rejoicing "as those that find great spoil" illustrates that knowing God's love surpasses every material windfall as the true ground of joy.

context: I will sing of the mercies of the Lord forever. With my mouth will I make known thy faithfulness to all generations.
point_summary: Psalm 89:1 is introduced as the sermon's foundational text, with the psalmist's vow to sing of God's mercies and proclaim His faithfulness to all generations read aloud as the opening declaration.

context: For I have said, mercy shall be built up forever. Thy faithfulness shalt thou establish in the very heavens.
point_summary: God's faithfulness is so enduring and heavenly in scope that it frames the entire sermon's opening call to receive his word with expectant hearts.

context: I have made a covenant with my chosen. I have sworn unto David, my servant.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Thy seed will I establish forever and build up thy throne to all generations. Selah.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And the heavens shall praise thy wonders, O Lord. Thy faithfulness also in the congregation of the saints.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: For who in the heaven can be compared unto the Lord? Who among the sons of the mighty can be likened unto the Lord?
point_summary: No created being in heaven can be compared to the Lord, establishing His absolute incomparability as the foundation for the psalm's praise of His faithfulness.

context: God is greatly to be feared in the assembly of the saints and to be had in reverence of all them that are about him.
point_summary: God's incomparable greatness demands reverent fear and awe from the assembly of His people, as no being in heaven or earth can be compared to Him.

context: O Lord God of hosts, who is a strong Lord like unto thee or to thy faithfulness round about thee?
point_summary: God's incomparable faithfulness—highlighted by the rhetorical question "who is a strong Lord like unto thee?"—frames the congregation's gathered need for his word and Spirit.

context: thy faithfulness to all generations
point_summary: Psalm 89's repeated refrain of God's faithfulness across its opening eight verses identifies it as a *maskil*—a psalm designed to instruct God's people in the truth that divine faithfulness underlies all human faithfulness.

context: thy faithfulness shalt thou establish in the very heavens
point_summary: The fourfold repetition of "faithfulness" in Psalm 89's opening eight verses signals that this instructional psalm (maskil) was written specifically to teach God's people about his faithfulness to them.

context: thy faithfulness also in the congregation of the saints
point_summary: The fourfold repetition of divine faithfulness in Psalm 89:1–8, including verse 5's reference to the congregation of the saints, identifies this maskil as God's intentional instruction to his people about his own faithfulness.

context: to thy faithfulness round about thee
point_summary: The fourfold repetition of divine faithfulness across Psalm 89's opening eight verses identifies this maskil's central instructional purpose: teaching God's people about his faithfulness to his own.

context: Do you know when God inspired Ethan to write Psalm 89?
point_summary: God chose to inspire Psalm 89's meditation on divine faithfulness precisely at the moment of Israel's greatest political catastrophe—the splitting of the kingdom under Rehoboam—not during its seasons of triumph.

context: I will sing of the mercies of the Lord forever. With my mouth will I make known thy faithfulness to all generations.
point_summary: God's unchanging faithfulness calls forth a perpetual, vocal response of praise and celebration, not complaint, even amid difficult circumstances.

context: I will sing of the mercies of the Lord forever
point_summary: God's sustained provision through Israel's wilderness years grounds the call to perpetual praise in Psalm 89:1, making ingratitude inexcusable for those who have witnessed his goodness.

context: if they break my statutes and keep not my commandments, then will I visit their transgression with the rod and their iniquity with stripes. Nevertheless, my loving kindness will I not utterly take from him, nor suffer my faithfulness to fail.
point_summary: Even when God disciplines his people's sin with the rod, his lovingkindness and faithfulness remain unrevoked, because human transgression cannot nullify the covenant promises he made to David.

context: thou art a priest forever, sorry, Psalm 110, after the order of Melchizedek
point_summary: Psalm 110's declaration that the Messiah would be "a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek" is presented as the Old Testament prophecy that the book of Hebrews systematically proves Christ fulfilled.

context: my son, today have I begotten thee in Psalm two
point_summary: Psalm 2:7's declaration "thou art my Son, today have I begotten thee" is cited as messianic prophecy fulfilled in Christ, paired with Psalm 110 to establish his superior, eternal priesthood.
Psalm 40:6–8, cited through Hebrews 10:5–9, reveals Christ as the fulfillment of the Old Testament sacrificial system, whose incarnate body accomplished what animal offerings could never achieve.
Christ's coming into the world fulfills the Old Testament plan for remission of sins, with Psalm 40:6–8 showing the Messiah as the one of whom the entire volume of Scripture speaks.

Messiah's coming into the world fulfilled what Psalm 40 declared—that he came not to offer animal sacrifices but to do God's will, accomplishing the remission of sins those sacrifices could never achieve.

context: as far as the east is from the west, so far hath He removed our transgressions from us
point_summary: God's pardon through Christ's blood removes transgressions as completely as the east is from the west, leaving no further offering for sin required.

context: if you looked at it, is Psalm 1611. And thy presence is fullness of joy.
point_summary: Delighting in God's presence brings fullness of joy, and as believers dwell there, God's desires progressively become their own desires.

reference_text: delight thyself also in the Lord... and He shall give thee the desires of thine heart
context: But we delight thyself also in the Lord. And as we delight ourselves in the Lord, notice it says, and He shall give thee the desires of thine heart.
point_summary: Delighting in the Lord transforms the believer's desires so that God's desires become our desires, aligning human will with divine will.

context: we actually see it in Psalm 21 and verse number 2, thou hast given him his heart's desire, and hast not withholding the request of his lips.
point_summary: Psalm 21:2 is cited as evidence that when believers delight themselves in the Lord, God's desires become their desires, aligning human longing with divine will.

context: commit thy way unto the Lord
point_summary: Committing one's way to the Lord, paired with trust, is presented as a necessary step in aligning personal will with God's will.

context: rest in the Lord
point_summary: Resting in the Lord is presented as the culminating step in a four-part pattern—trust, delight, commit, rest—by which a believer aligns personal will with God's will.

context: God gives us here in Psalm 37 in verses 3 through 7
point_summary: Trusting, delighting, committing, and resting in the Lord—drawn from Psalm 37:3–7—are presented as the four progressive steps by which a believer's will becomes aligned with God's will.

context: God gives us here in Psalm 37 in verses 3 through 7
point_summary: Delighting in the Lord transforms the believer's desires to align with God's, so that praying "thy will be done" becomes a natural expression of a surrendered heart rather than a mere cliche.

context: God gives us here in Psalm 37 in verses 3 through 7
point_summary: Committing one's way to the Lord, as Psalm 37:5 commands, is presented as one of four practical steps by which a believer aligns personal will with God's will through trust and surrender.

context: God gives us here in Psalm 37 in verses 3 through 7
point_summary: Delighting in, trusting, committing to, and resting in the Lord are presented as the progressive steps by which a believer's will becomes aligned with God's will.

context: God gives us here in Psalm 37 in verses 3 through 7
point_summary: Resting in the Lord is presented as the culminating posture—alongside trusting, delighting, and committing—by which a believer aligns personal will with God's will.

context: Do you remember Psalm 37 twice in those verses of Scripture, trust in the Lord.
point_summary: Psalm 37's repeated call to "trust in the Lord" is invoked to reinforce that praying "thy will be done" is an act of recognizing God's way and will as best.

context: Oh God, thou art my God. Early will I seek thee. My soul thirsteth for thee. My flesh longeth for thee in a dry and thirsty land where no water is.
point_summary: The elementary yet searching question raised by David's early, thirsting pursuit of God is not whether believers know this discipline, but whether they have actually appropriated it into their daily lives.

context: My soul shall be satisfied as with marrow and fatness, and my mouth shall praise thee with joyful lips.
point_summary: Soul-satisfaction in God — depicted in verse 5 as feasting on marrow and fatness — is presented as something that must move from mere knowledge into the practiced discipline of daily life.

context: When I remember thee upon my bed and meditate on thee in the night watches,
point_summary: Meditating on God through the night watches is presented as a spiritual discipline that must move beyond mere knowledge into the practiced rhythms of daily life.

context: because thou has been my help, therefore in the shadow of thy wings will I rejoice.
point_summary: Rejoicing in God's shadow is grounded in remembered help — the discipline of recalling past divine aid fuels present delight in Him.

context: My soul followeth hard after thee. Thy right hand upholdeth me.
point_summary: Clinging to God as a daily discipline—not merely as known doctrine—is what it means for the soul to follow hard after him.

context: Seek the Lord and his strength. Seek his face forevermore.
point_summary: Revival is rightly understood as a renewed seeking of the Lord, with Psalm 105:4's command to seek His face "forevermore" illustrating the persistent, intentional orientation that revived hearts require.

context: Let all those that seek thee rejoice and be glad in thee. Let such as love thy salvation say continually, the Lord be magnified.
point_summary: Revival is rightly understood as a renewed seeking of the Lord, with Psalm 40:16 cited as scriptural warrant that those who seek God are characterized by rejoicing and magnifying Him.

context: Blessed are they that keep his testimonies and that seek him with the whole heart.
point_summary: Revival is rightly understood as a renewed seeking of the Lord, with Psalm 119:2's call to seek Him wholeheartedly framing the believer's intentional pursuit of God over the pressures of life.

context: When we come to Psalm 63, David is not in the palace. When we come to Psalm 63, David is not in the temple rejoicing as the Levites sing hymns of worship. When we come to Psalm 63, I want you to understand as it says in the inscription in the psalm, he was in the wilderness of Judah.
point_summary: Psalm 63 illustrates that genuine revival begins in the individual heart, as David's intentional pursuit of God amid the wilderness pressure of Absalom's rebellion models seeking the Lord as life's first priority.

context: O God, thou art my God, early will I seek thee, my soul thirsteth for thee, my flesh longeth for thee in a dry and thirsty land where no water is.
point_summary: Spiritual complacency—crowded out by schedule and media—has displaced the fervent, early-morning thirst for God that Psalm 63:1 describes as the soul's deepest and only true need.

reference_text: God inhabits the praise of his people
context: I believe that God, he inhabits the praise of his people.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I will lift up mine eyes into the hills from whence cometh my help.
point_summary: Creation's greatness points to a personal helper, as Psalm 121:1 is cited to show that the God who made heaven and earth is the same God who comes to our aid.

context: My help cometh from the Lord, which made heaven and earth.
point_summary: God's greatness as Creator grounds the believer's confidence in Him as personal helper, as declared in the psalmist's words that help comes from the Lord who made heaven and earth.

context: Turn over to Psalm 12, it's where we were going to begin this morning before they sang that song.
point_summary: God's promise to preserve His word is explicitly stated in Psalm 12:7, countering the claim that Scripture contains no such preservation promise.

reference_text: Psalm 12 in verses six and seven
context: But look here in Psalm 12 in verses six and seven. If you're taking notes this morning, number one, the promise of preservation. The Bible says this in verse six, the words of the Lord are pure words.
point_summary: God's words are pure, without error, and trustworthy—a purity the psalmist illustrates through the image of silver refined seven times in a furnace.

context: as silver tried in a furnace of earth, purified seven times
point_summary: God's words are pure — without error, trustworthy, and reliable — a purity illustrated by silver refined seven times, representing the highest standard of purity achievable.

context: thou shalt keep them, oh Lord. Thou shalt, what does it say? Preserve them from this generation forever
point_summary: God's promise in Psalm 12:7 to preserve His words "from this generation forever" is a commitment only He can fulfill, because He alone is eternal.

context: This promise that we find in Psalm 12 in verse number seven with regard to the truth of preservation is a precious promise that we hold dear and we rejoice in this promise.
point_summary: God's eternal nature is the sole basis for the preservation promise in verse 7, since only an eternal God can guarantee His Word will be kept "from this generation forever."

context: forever, O Lord, thy word is settled in heaven
point_summary: God's eternal settlement of His word in heaven is cited as evidence that His promise of preservation has already been fulfilled, despite ongoing attempts to alter Scripture.

context: thy word is true from the beginning. And then notice, and every one of thy righteous judgments endureth, how long? Forever.
point_summary: God's word being "true from the beginning" and enduring "forever" grounds present confidence in Scripture's preservation, since the promise's reliability rests entirely on the character of the One who made it.

context: In Psalm 100, we look at verse number five. Notice what the Bible says, for the Lord is good.
point_summary: God's goodness and everlasting mercy are grounded in His unchanging nature, not in human circumstances, making preservation of His Word an expression of His character.

reference_text: taste and see that the Lord is good
context: The psalmist had declared earlier, taste and see that the Lord is good.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: it says that his mercy is everlasting, but then notice in verse number five, and his truth endureth to all generations.
point_summary: God's preservation of his Word is inseparable from his character, since the same verse that declares his goodness and everlasting mercy also promises his truth endures to all generations.

reference_text: I will preserve it from this generation forever
context: He said, I will preserve it, I will preserve it from this generation forever.
point_summary: Divine preservation of Scripture is the necessary foundation for biblical authority—without confidence that God has kept His Word intact across generations, the way of salvation itself becomes uncertain.

context: Turn to the book of Psalms, if you will, in Psalm chapter 19, Psalm chapter 19. And I want to look at just a couple of passages here.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the law of the Lord is perfect, converting the soul. The testimony of the Lord is sure, making wise the simple.
point_summary: God's Word is to be desired above gold because its perfections—converting the soul, making wise the simple, rejoicing the heart—make obedience a gift to be treasured, not a burden to be endured.

context: I rejoice at thy word. Lord, as one that findeth great spoil.
point_summary: Psalm 119:162's image of rejoicing "as one that findeth great spoil" is used to call believers to approach Scripture with the same excited, grateful joy a person feels upon discovering great treasure.

context: it's to be desired more than gold, it's sweeter than honey in the honeycomb
point_summary: God's Word surpasses even the most ardent human pursuit of wealth, and the believer who grasps what Scripture truly is will desire it more than gold.

context: moreover, by them is thy servant warned. I'm thankful for the warnings, and in keeping of them, obeying them, is great reward.
point_summary: Obedience to God's Word brings great reward, and its warnings—like posted cautions on a road or product labels—exist to protect the servant who heeds them.

context: though the heathen rage, God is in control
point_summary: Though the heathen rage, Psalm 2 is invoked to affirm that God remains sovereignly in control of world events, ruling and reigning victoriously despite appearances to the contrary.

context: Go over to Psalm 100, if you will. The 100th Psalm is a familiar Psalm as well.
point_summary: Psalm 100 is brought alongside 1 Thessalonians 5:18 to show that thankfulness is not passive but requires active, commanded response—"make," "serve," "come," "know."

context: Make a joyful noise unto the Lord, all ye lands.
point_summary: Psalm 100 is brought alongside 1 Thessalonians 5:18 to ground thanksgiving in the unchanging goodness and everlasting mercy of God, which hold regardless of circumstances.

context: Serve the Lord with gladness. Come before his presence with singing.
point_summary: Serving the Lord with gladness grounds the call to thanksgiving in God's unchanging goodness and everlasting mercy, which hold firm regardless of present trials or circumstances.

context: Know ye that the Lord, he is God. It is he that hath made us, and not we ourselves. We are his people and the sheep of his pasture.
point_summary: Gratitude toward God is grounded in the unchanging goodness of the Lord, whose mercy endures regardless of present circumstances or trials.

context: Enter into his gates with thanksgiving, and into his courts with praise. Be thankful unto him and bless his name.
point_summary: Entering God's presence with thanksgiving is grounded in the unchanging goodness and everlasting mercy of God, which hold true regardless of circumstances or trials.

context: For the Lord is good; his mercy is everlasting; and his truth endureth to all generations.
point_summary: God's enduring goodness and everlasting mercy, declared in Psalm 100:5, are presented as the foundational reason why thanksgiving is owed to God regardless of circumstances or trials.

context: in his presence there is fullness of joy
point_summary: Entering God's presence—where fullness of joy is found—is the prerequisite that enables a Spirit-filled believer to obey the command to give thanks in everything.

context: enter into his gates with thanksgiving
point_summary: Entering God's presence with thanksgiving, as Psalm 100:4 commands, is the posture that enables a Spirit-filled believer to obey the call to give thanks in everything.

context: the psalmist tells us that God daily loatheth us with benefits
point_summary: Daily God loads believers with benefits and blessings, grounding the command to give thanks in the concrete reality of his ongoing provision rather than in favorable circumstances.

context: in his presence, his fullness of joy
point_summary: Fullness of joy found in God's presence, cited from Psalm 16, grounds Tozer's longing to want God more and frames the sermon's call to desire deeper communion.

reference_text: restore unto me the joy of thy salvation
context: as David said, restore unto me the joy of thy salvation
point_summary: Restoration of joy lost to sin is the need voiced in Lamentations 5:21's "renew our days," with David's petition in Psalm 51:12 cited as the pattern for asking God to restore that joy.

context: remember David said, taste and see that the Lord is good
point_summary: Tasting and seeing that the Lord is good (Ps. 34:8), cited via Tozer, grounds the sermon's call to renewed desire for God—the turning Jeremiah prays for in Lamentations 5:21.

context: It is good for me to draw near to thee, O God.
point_summary: Drawing near to God is declared good, echoing Psalm 73:28, as the closing prayer calls individuals to honest self-evaluation and renewed closeness with the Lord.

context: The heathen rage, right? I mean, they set up their plans on how they're going to the kings of the earth, how they're going to overthrow, and God shall have them in derision. He laughs at them. Psalm 2, right?
point_summary: Psalm 2's portrait of raging nations and God laughing their schemes to derision grounds the assurance that world events are not falling apart but falling into place under God's sovereign rule.

context: this morning, Psalm 119 this morning, as we continue with our series here on Sunday mornings, Great is the Lord.
point_summary: God's greatness, already established through the inspiration and preservation of Scripture, now finds personal expression in the psalmist's declaration of love for the law in Psalm 119:97.

context: O how love I thy law! It thy law is my meditation all the day.
point_summary: The psalmist's exclamation "O how love I thy law!" is presented as a heartfelt declaration of love for God's word, with the sermon focusing on how that love is expressed rather than merely claimed.

context: O how love I thy law!
point_summary: Loving God's law is inseparable from loving God himself, as the psalmist's exclamation in verse 97 frames meditation on Scripture as a natural expression of genuine devotion to the Lord.

context: O love the Lord, all ye his saints

context: This morning, as we look at this 119th psalm, and we'll look at a number of verses, if you're taking notes, I want you to notice with me number one this morning, and really there's only one number one, there's no other points this morning, there's a bunch of sub-points. context: oh, how love I thy law

context: therefore, I love thy commandments above gold, yea, above fine gold

context: I love thy commandments above gold, yea, above fine gold
point_summary: The psalmist's love for God's Word surpasses even gold and fine gold (Ps. 119:127), illustrating that Scripture holds greater value than the most prized earthly treasure.

context: I love thy commandments above gold
point_summary: Loving God's commandments above gold reflects the psalmist's conviction that Scripture holds greater value than the most prized earthly treasure.

context: I rejoice at thy word as one that findeth great spoil
point_summary: Rejoicing over God's Word as one who finds great spoil illustrates that the psalmist valued Scripture above gold, treating it as discovered treasure rather than a routine possession.

context: Thy word is very pure, therefore thy servant loveth it.

context: My soul hath kept thy testimonies, and I love them exceedingly.

context: oh, how love I thy law, it is my meditation all the day

context: Thou, through thy commandments, has made me wiser than mine enemies, for they are ever with me

context: I have more understanding than all my teachers, for thy testimonies are my meditation

context: Blessed is the man that walketh not in the counsel of the ungodly, nor standeth in the way of sinners, nor sitteth in the seat of the scornful

context: But his delight, his delight is in the law of the Lord, and in his law doth he meditate day and night

context: yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil. And then the next phrase is the phrase in which I wanna focus on our attention this morning, for thou art with me, thy rod and thy staff, they comfort me

context: Thou art with me
point_summary: God's presence with his people—affirmed in the phrase "thou art with me"—is the source of comfort amid fear and anxiety, just as a parent's presence calms a frightened child.

context: Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil. There's not a period there, it's not that I'm not gonna fear evil just because I'm this person that's not gonna be afraid, no, it's because thou art with me. Thy rod and thy staff, they comfort me.

context: I will fear no evil as I pass through the valley of the shadow of death. Why? Thou art with me

context: Psalm 141, in verse number three, notice what the psalmist says, set a watch, O Lord, before my mouth. Keep the door of my lips.

context: Set a watch, O Lord, before my mouth. Keep the door of my lips.

context: I think often of the truth in Psalm 118, we see the psalmist there, and of course, the psalm is all about the Bible

context: before the mountains were brought forth, or ever thou hast formed the earth and the world, even from everlasting to everlasting, thou art God

context: verse one begins with the personal praise and exaltation of the Lord. I will bless the Lord at all times

context: We are his people in the sheep of his pasture

context: Entered to his gates with thanksgiving and into his courts with praise
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: thou called and I answered from Psalm 81

context: In Psalm 97 and verse number 12, the psalmist gives the admonition to the people of God to rejoice, to give thanks at the remembrance of God's holiness.

context: Psalm chapter number 50, as we look at this verse, and verse number 15 will be the verse that we'll be looking at this evening. Psalm 50 and verse number 15
point_summary: Psalm 50:15 is presented as a verse whose full scope will be expounded as a source of encouragement, comfort, and strengthening for God's people throughout the sermon.

context: And call upon me in the day of trouble, I will deliver thee, and thou shalt glorify me
point_summary: God's instruction in Psalm 50:15 demands active, heeded response—a divine call to prayer in trouble that, like any set of directions, only benefits those who actually follow it.

context: O my people, and I will speak, O Israel, and I will testify against thee. Notice this, I am God, even thy God.
point_summary: God's self-identification — "I am God, even thy God" — establishes his sovereign ownership of all creation as the foundation for why he alone is worthy to be called upon in trouble.

context: For every beast of the forest is mine, and the cattle upon a thousand hills
point_summary: God's ownership of every beast of the forest and the cattle on a thousand hills establishes that He needs nothing from man, making His invitation to call upon Him in trouble an act of pure grace rather than transaction.

context: I know all the fowls of the mountains and the wild beasts of the field are mine
point_summary: God's omniscient ownership of all creation — every bird and wild beast — establishes that He needs nothing from man, grounding the call to prayer in verse 15 on His sufficiency rather than human offering.

context: And call upon me in the day of trouble. I will deliver thee, and thou shalt glorify me
point_summary: Verses 7–14 establish God's absolute ownership of all creation as the theological foundation for why calling upon him in the day of trouble (v. 15) is both meaningful and answered.

context: David speaks about this, about all his troubles
point_summary: Psalm 34 is cited as David's own testimony about "all his troubles," grounding the sermon's point that trouble is a universal human reality God fully knows and acknowledges.

reference_text: call upon me in the day of trouble
context: when God says, and call upon me in the day of trouble, He absolutely is available
point_summary: God's invitation in Psalm 50:15 to call upon Him in trouble is sincere and without limit—unlike human offers of help, He is always available and always able to act.

context: thou callest in trouble and I delivered. I delivered thee, he says. I answered thee in the secret place of thunder. I proved thee at the waters of Meribah Silah

context: God delivered him out of all his afflictions

context: Turn over to Psalm 34, if you will. Psalm chapter 34.

context: I will bless the Lord at all times. His praise shall continually be in my mouth.
point_summary: Psalm 34:1's declaration that David would bless the Lord "at all times" frames his praise as inseparable from his experience of divine deliverance in trouble, illustrating Psalm 50:15's promise in practice.

context: My soul shall make her boast in the Lord. The humble shall hear thereof and be glad.

context: Oh, magnify the Lord with me and let us exalt His name together.

context: They looked unto Him and were lightened and their faces were not ashamed.

context: this poor man cried and the Lord heard him and saved him out of all his troubles.

point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: but I trusted in Thee
point_summary: David's declaration "I trusted in Thee" is set against a backdrop of severe, sustained trouble, making the confession of trust remarkable precisely because the circumstances demand an explanation for it.

context: Have mercy upon me, O Lord, for I am in trouble
point_summary: Verse 9's declaration "I am in trouble" establishes the severe, genuine crisis—likely Absalom's rebellion—that makes David's confession of trust in verse 14 necessary and meaningful.

context: For my life is spent with grief, my years with sighing, my strength faileth because of mine iniquity, all my bones are consumed
point_summary: David's confession that his life is "spent with grief," his strength failing, and his bones consumed establishes the depth of his affliction as the necessary backdrop for understanding why his declaration of trust in verse 14 is so striking.

context: my times are in His hands
point_summary: David's confession that "my times are in His hands" grounds trust in God's sovereign control over every shifting season of stress, whether personal, national, or circumstantial.

context: his times, David's, were in God's hands
point_summary: David's declaration that "my times are in thy hand" anchors the believer's trust in God's sovereignty over every season of change and instability that marks human life.

context: I love what David says in verse 14. Thou art my God.
point_summary: David's declaration "Thou art my God" grounds the sermon's move from God's sovereign control of history to his personal, knowable relationship with individual believers.

context: But I trusted in thee
point_summary: Personal trust in a sovereign and personal God is the believer's appointed role — not running the universe, but anchoring inner stability amid chaos by saying, "I trusted in thee."

context: thou art my God, my times are lived in my hands
point_summary: Confessing "my times are in thy hand" moves trust from a comfortable phrase to a total abandonment of control over life, family, and circumstances to God.

context: my times are lived in God's hands. Thou art my God
point_summary: Declaring "my times are in thy hand," the sermon calls believers to surrender every hard season—illness, financial crisis, pandemic—into God's sovereign keeping, finding peace precisely because their times belong to him.

context: I trust it in thee
point_summary: Trusting God with one's times — in crisis, illness, or loss — means resting in the conviction that every season of life is held in his hands.

context: but I'm trusting in thee
point_summary: Declaring "but I trusted in thee" is the defining confession of the believer's life, anchoring every season—crisis or calm—in the conviction that one's times are held in God's sovereign hands.

context: the entrance of thy words giveth light
point_summary: Scripture's own testimony that God's Word gives light explains why spiritual enemies throughout history have sought to silence, suppress, and destroy it.

context: I, the Lord, hears the cry of the poor and needy
point_summary: God's promise never to leave or forsake His people is confirmed even in cognitive decline, as one elderly believer retains no memory except that single assurance.

context: Wilt thou not revive us again that thy people may rejoice in thee
point_summary: Psalm 85:6 is introduced as the defining revival text, with its cry "Wilt thou not revive us again" framing the sermon's central question of whether God's people are prepared for revival.

context: The psalmist says, search me O Lord and show me if there be any wicked way in me. Right? In Psalm 139.
point_summary: Repentance begins with inviting God's searching gaze — citing Psalm 139:23 as the posture of one who asks God to expose hidden sin as the first step toward revival readiness.

context: wash me throughly from mine iniquity and cleanse me from my sin
point_summary: David's plea in Psalm 51:2 to be washed *throughly* (completely) from iniquity is presented as a deeply personal, individual confession — the necessary posture for anyone who wants revival.

context: if you read the entirety of Psalm 51
point_summary: Psalm 51's intensely personal, first-person confession — "wash me," "cleanse me" — distinguishes individual repentance from corporate confession, making it the model for the personal dealing with sin revival requires.

context: Sweet in thy presence is fullness of joy. Psalm 1611.
point_summary: Fullness of joy in God's presence, drawn from Psalm 16:11, is held up as the sweetness of restored communion that comes only when known sin is honestly confessed and cleared away.

context: Psalm 1611, in thy presence is fullness of joy
point_summary: Fullness of joy is found in God's presence alone, calling believers to set aside busyness and simply draw near to Him in worship without petition.

context: it is good for us to draw nigh to God
point_summary: Drawing near to God is presented as genuinely good for the believer, grounding the revival-readiness call to seek God's presence in the psalmist's own testimony.

context: If I regard iniquity in my heart, the Lord will not hear me
point_summary: Cherished sin blocks both prayer and God's hearing, because regarding iniquity in the heart causes the Lord not to hear and drives the believer away from prayer altogether.

context: if I regard iniquity in my heart, the Lord will not hear me
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: But verily God hath heard me. He hath attended to the voice of my prayer
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Blessed be the God which hath not turned away my prayer, nor his mercy from me
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: if someone regards an iniquity, he will not hear me
point_summary: Regarding iniquity in the heart does not prevent God from audibly hearing prayer, but—drawing on the Hebrew *shama*—it removes His inclination to be moved and respond.

context: When I regard iniquity, I see it. I know it's there. I allow it to stay there
point_summary: To "regard iniquity in the heart" means not merely stumbling into sin but knowingly allowing it to remain, rationalizing its presence rather than genuinely clearing it out before God.

context: If I regard iniquity in my heart, the Lord will not hear me
point_summary: The personal pronouns I, my, and me in Psalm 66:18 establish that harboring iniquity is a self-inflicted condition of the heart, not something imposed by others.

context: when we regard iniquity, when we accept it as who we are and we're satisfied, God says I'm unmoved
point_summary: Cherished, unrepented sin renders prayer ineffective because God will not act in ways that would amount to approving the very iniquity the petitioner has chosen to retain.

context: But verily God hath heard me
point_summary: Psalm 66:19 is presented as the psalmist's testimony that after confessing and removing regarded iniquity from his heart, God heard his prayer and mercifully did not withhold His presence.

context: I've regarded iniquity in my heart. I know what it is for God not to hear me
point_summary: Regarding iniquity in the heart blocks God's responsiveness to prayer, but God's mercy can move a believer to confess and forsake that sin, restoring divine hearing.

context: verily God hath heard me
point_summary: God's mercy — not human merit — is what moves a believer from unheard prayer back to restored communion, as the psalmist testifies that confession and forsaking accepted sin brought God to hear him again.

context: If a man regards iniquity in his heart the Lord will not hear him
point_summary: Regarding iniquity in the heart blocks God's hearing, but honest confession and the effort to forsake sin—however halting—opens the way for God's mercy to respond.

context: God hath heard my prayer
point_summary: God's attentiveness in Psalm 66:19 is the promised counterpart to the forsaking of iniquity: when believers honestly confess and work to empty their hearts, God in mercy will hear.

context: we've tasted and we've seen that the Lord is good
point_summary: Tasting and seeing that the Lord is good (Ps. 34:8) grounds the call to praise without shame, since personal experience of God's goodness removes all embarrassment in declaring his worthiness.

context: our God daily loadeth us with benefits
point_summary: Daily God loads His redeemed people with benefits, making their ingratitude—whether outright complaint or silent neglect of thanksgiving—all the more inexcusable and unworthy of Him.

context: the Lord delivered me out of all my troubles
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: verse number one, the Lord is my shepherd
point_summary: The personal claim "the LORD is my shepherd" grounds an evangelistic appeal: Christ the good, great, and chief shepherd died to pay for sins entirely by his own work, making salvation freely available.

context: We're to enter into his gates with thanksgiving
point_summary: Entering God's presence with thanksgiving, as Psalm 100:4 calls for, is briefly invoked to affirm that believers ought to be a praising, grateful people, as Paul's own doxology in verse 16 exemplifies.

context: search me, oh God, and know me
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Notice it with me in verse one, Psalm 37. Again, a Psalm of David. Fret not thyself because of evildoers, neither be thou envious against the workers of iniquity, for they shall soon be cut down like the grass and wither as the green herb.
point_summary: Psalm 37:1's command "fret not" is presented as a direct, timely word to a generation characterized by anxiety and worry over the prosperity of evildoers.

context: Trust in the Lord and do good. So shalt thou dwell in the land and verily thou shalt be fed.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Delight thyself also in the Lord and he shall give thee the desires of thine heart.
point_summary: Delighting in the Lord is presented as the condition upon which God grants the desires of the heart, set within a series of commands countering a fretful generation.

context: Commit thy way unto the Lord. Trust also in him and he shall bring it to pass.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And he shall bring forth thy righteousness as the light and thy judgment as the noonday
point_summary: God's promise to vindicate the righteous is pictured as light breaking forth and judgment shining as the noonday, offered here as reason to trust rather than fret.

context: Rest in the Lord and wait patiently for him. Fret not thyself because of him who prospereth in his way, because of the man who bringeth wicked devices to pass
point_summary: Fretting is presented as a generational spiritual failure that Psalm 37 directly addresses, with verse 7's commands to rest and wait patiently set within a passage calling God's people away from anxiety over the wicked.

context: Cease from anger and forsake wrath. Fret not thyself in any wise to do evil
point_summary: Fretting, the psalm warns, leads toward evil — so God commands his people to cease from anger and forsake wrath before fretfulness corrupts their testimony and conduct.

context: Verse one tells us not to be envious against the workers of iniquity
point_summary: The Hebrew word translated "fret not" carries the sense of being so consumed by anger or fear that it alters one's countenance, perspective, and response—displacing the Spirit's guidance with reactive emotion.

context: For they shall soon be cut down like the grass and wither as the green herb
point_summary: The apparent prosperity of the wicked is a momentary snapshot, not the full story — verse 2 assures that they will ultimately be cut down like grass, and life always works out as God says it will.

context: Trust in the Lord and do good. So shalt thou dwell in the land, and verily thou shalt be fed.
point_summary: Trusting God (v. 3) means taking complete refuge in him—placing confidence entirely beyond one's own ingenuity—rather than panicking when his plan feels slow or his purposes remain hidden.

context: delight thyself also in the Lord and he shall give thee the desires of thine heart
point_summary: Delighting in the Lord is widely misread as a promise to fulfill personal wishes, but the sermon flags this as one of Scripture's most misunderstood verses.

context: Delight yourself, Lord, and He'll give you whatever you want
point_summary: Delighting in the Lord transforms the promise of Psalm 37:4 from a wish-granting formula into an invitation to want God himself as the supreme desire of one's heart.

context: commit your way unto the Lord in verse 5. Commit thy way unto the Lord. Trust also in Him and He shall bring it to pass.
point_summary: Committing one's way to the Lord (v. 5) means rolling one's direction together with God's until the two become one—purposing to follow wherever He leads.
